>> A possible solution for the numerous init arguments of the LSTMLayer can be that you handle the gates and the cell as separate classes (or instances thereof). This would remove a lot of duplicated code. It may also make the whole thing more readable.

> I'm having trouble picturing what you mean by this, can you be more specific?

you could e.g. do something like this:

```python
class Cell(object):
    def __init__(self, W_in=init.Normal(0.1),
                 W_hid=init.Normal(0.1),
                 b=init.Normal(0.1),
                 nonlinearity=nonlinearities.sigmoid):

class Gate(object):
    def __init__(self, W_in=init.Normal(0.1),
                 W_hid=init.Normal(0.1),
                 W_cell=init.Normal(0.1),
                 b=init.Normal(0.1),
                 nonlinearity=nonlinearities.tanh):
    
class LSTMLayer(Layer):
    def __init__(self, incoming, num_units,
                 ingate=Gate(),
                 forgetgate=Gate(),
                 cell=Cell(),
                 outgate=Gate(), ...):
```
Then there would be way fewer arguments in the `__init__` method of the `LSTMLayer`. `Cell` could be of course just a `Gate` with another nonlinearity and `W_cell` set to `None` (or `Gate` extending the `Cell` class). No peepholes could be realised easily by setting `W_cell` to `None`.

We'd just need to split the check on [this line](https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/f4ec2383277fcc5172313c2585d23b653f153306/streamparse/storm/bolt.py#L366) out into its own method called `BatchingBolt.is_batch_ready()` (or something like that).  We could then just have the default implementation be:

```python
def is_batch_ready(self):
    return self._tick_counter > self.ticks_between_batches and self._batches
```

Then in your `ThresholdBatchingBolt` subclass you would just need to override that one method as:

```python
def is_batch_ready(self):
    if self._batches:
        num_tuples = sum(len(batch) for batch in itervalues(self._batches))
        return (num_tuples >= self.batch_threshold or 
                super(ThresholdBatchingBolt, self).is_batch_ready())
    else:
        return False
```

That'll give you an implementation that will fallback on time if the size threshold isn't met, so you just need to make sure you set `ticks_between_batches` high enough.

In my understanding, except `last_match` in #35 has only 2 possible types of value: either `None` or a string value of the last trigger; for all other user vars, they have 3 possible types of value: 

1. `"undefined"` means they are not yet initialised (i.e. name undefined). 

2. `None` has two meanings, either the key does not exist, or there is no value to show (i.e. history = None meaning that there is no history of the discourse yet). 

3. A "usual" value of the variable (i.e. `age == 5` or `name == Alice`)

If so, according to your explanation, there are two additional meaning of these terms. 

1. `"undefined"` means a "usual" value (the 3rd use above) when variable was literally set to the word "undefined". As you said, API functions can't tell whether the variable really doesn't exist or not. Do we aim to solve this or accept this as a limitation of the API? 

2.  `None` also implies deletion of a variable or subset of variables. Otherwise, we'd have to delete by `clear_uservars()`. Broadly: I'm in favor of a wrapper client (`EasyClient`) that abstracts away some of the details of each API and provides a very simple "I want a certificate, please get me one" API, which I think is what @bmw's proposing. I think the MultiVersionClient in this PR doesn't get us there, since you have to choose which methods to call based on what API version you're using.

>  then need to split the certificate and chain, probably by looking for the first line containing "END CERTIFICATE".

Certbot should actually parse the PEM chunks and ensure they are of the correct type (CERTIFICATE) before passing them along to the filesystem.

> We need a method like register/new_account that also allows the user of the class to prompt the user to agree to the TOS.

In ACMEv1, Boulder already provides `"meta": {"terms-of-service": "https://...."}`. In ACMEv2, this is called `termsOfService`, but we can provide a simple shim to pick whichever is present. So our new `EasyClient` would have a method to get the terms of service URL before you register, in order to present it to the user. There would be another method, `register`, that would either perform the two-step dance from ACMEv1 or the one-step dance from ACMEv2.

There's a bit of a corner case for the people who have done private non-Boulder implementations of ACMEv1, in case they don't provide `"meta": {"terms-of-service": "..."}`, but we can reach out to them; I think there are only a couple.

Some notes:

- `ssl_dhparam` takes a file as a parameter
- The location of this file is either distribution-dependent or flag-dependent, and either way we can't know it until runtime.
- The directive, and the therefore the location of the file, should ideally go into `options-ssl-nginx.conf`.
- Ergo, the contents of `options-ssl-nginx.conf` are only knowable at runtime.
- We therefore cannot, pre-distribution, compute the hash of `options-ssl-nginx.conf`.
- We cannot distribute the hash of the file we would have written in a previous version, because we can only compute that at runtime

So either:
- We need a new mechanism for pushing updates to `options-ssl-nginx.conf`, or
- The `ssl_dhparam` doesn't go into `options-ssl-nginx.conf`

Thanks for opening this!

If the proxy is stripping or changing the Host header, all API endpoints should be affected. If there's an action to take on this, it might be to balk at any request missing the Host header. It's not technically a [valid HTTP request](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html#sec14.23) if it doesn't have a Host header. Firefox has the quirk that it doesn't send Origin when it would have the same value as Host, which has caused us security issues in the past. The issue here seems to have been proxy configuration which stripped the Host header, producing invalid requests. Preserving the original Host header in the proxy ought to be the right thing to do.

The JupyterHub docs [include working configurations for nginx and apache](https://jupyterhub.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/config-examples.html#nginx), which should ensure correct proxied headers. Apache requires explicitly setting PreserveHostHeader, while nginx's default behavior has been sufficient in this regard in my experience.

You mean support `ini` for the `.sops.yaml` configuration file documented at https://github.com/mozilla/sops#using-sops-yaml-conf-to-select-kms-pgp-for-new-files ?

I don't think that's a problem as long as the loaded configuration fits into the same dictionary. But, to be honest, that `yaml` formal is so trivial I don't really see a benefit to supporting `ini` as well.

In short, no. Currently sops simply calls whichever `gpg` binary it finds in `PATH`, so that will be GNUPG, most likely. Aliases won't work as sops (correctly) doesn't use a shell to run pgp. There are a few possible solutions:

- Link `qubes-gpg-client-wrapper` to some `gpg` and add the directory where that binary lives to `PATH` so that it's found before GNU's one.
- Slightly more desirable, file a PR so that instead of using `gpg`, we can use any other binary name, so that anything with `gpg`'s interface works.

For context, I'm running `detox` with `watchdog` on every python file change, with the idea being that if I break a test, I'll know roughly as soon as I hit save.  The aim is to have the console go red before I can go and refresh my browser.  The immediacy of feedback leads to a very smooth-feeling workflow.

>The real place where venv-update shines is when things change 

For sure, it's faster than `--recreate`, which is why I'm keeping it.  My workflow for changing my dependencies now, though, is a bit clunky.  I update `requirements.txt`, then uncomment `tox_pip_extensions_ext_venv_update = true`, then run `detox` (usually by saving an open `.py` file), then recomment the line.

From the above, you'll see why I think it should be simple to automate the process - I'm already telling it when to or not to short-circuit.  It's entirely possible I'm a huge idiot and there's something simple I'm missing somewhere, of course.

There are a couple "ah-ha!" moments I had when reviewing the RFCs and the code.
1) The syntax for LDAP filters matching GUIDs is *not* really special.  What makes creating the filter so non-intuitive is that GUIDs are stored as *binary* data.  The string representations we commonly work with are not how the data is stored, and that is why trying to filter on the common string representation doesn't work.
2) [RFC 4515](https://tools.ietf.org/search/rfc4515) explains how escaping in LDAP filters works.  In particular, on page 4, the characters that are normally escaped.  It further goes on to say:

> Other octets that are part of the \<normal\> set may be escaped using this mechanism, for example, non-printing ASCII characters.

And later:

> As indicated by the \<valueencoding\> rule, implementations MUST escape all octets greater than 0x7F that are not part of a valid UTF-8 encoding sequence when they generate a string representation of a search filter.

This tells us that binary values more or less need to be represented in the escaped format, which is what the Ping Identity article you referenced is describing.  (More on the "more or less" later).

3) The `pureldap.LDAPFilter_*` objects have both `str()` representations and `asTest()` methods.  The former is used when transmitting the BER sequences to an LDAP server.  The latter is used when displaying textual representations of the data (during debugging, for example).

A code example may get to the heart of the matter:

```Python
>>> import ldaptor
>>> import ldaptor.ldapfilter as lf
>>> guid_chars = "90395F191AB51B4A9E9686C66CB18D11"
>>> chars = [c for c in guid_chars]
>>> chars
['9', '0', '3', '9', '5', 'F', '1', '9', '1', 'A', 'B', '5', '1', 'B', '4', 'A', '9', 'E', '9', '6', '8', '6', 'C', '6', '6', 'C', 'B', '1', '8', 'D', '1', '1']
>>> firsts = chars[::2]
>>> seconds = chars[1::2]
>>> pairs = zip(firsts, seconds)
>>> escaped_guid_str_rep = ''.join([r"\{0}{1}".format(a, b) for a, b in pairs])
>>> print(escaped_guid_str_rep)
\90\39\5F\19\1A\B5\1B\4A\9E\96\86\C6\6C\B1\8D\11
>>> filter_str = "(guid={0})".format(escaped_guid_str_rep)
>>> print(filter_str)
(guid=\90\39\5F\19\1A\B5\1B\4A\9E\96\86\C6\6C\B1\8D\11)
>>> fo = lf.parseFilter(filter_str)
>>> str(fo)
'\xa3\x18\x04\x04guid\x04\x10\x909_\x19\x1a\xb5\x1bJ\x9e\x96\x86\xc6l\xb1\x8d\x11'
>>> fo.asText()
'(guid=\x909_\x19\x1a\xb5\x1bJ\x9e\x96\x86\xc6l\xb1\x8d\x11)'
>>> chr(0x39)
'9'
>>> chr(0x5F)
'_'
>>> 
```

What we see here is that the filter string will be parsed and represented correctly both as bytes sent to the LDAP server and as text displayed during debugging.  However, the textual representation used for debugging, while correct, seems a bit odd to us as human beings because it mixes *escaped* and *non-escaped* representations.  All the bytes that *must* be escaped are, but the parts that may *optionally* be escaped in a representation are not.

This is actually a bit confusing to look at, but the sequences:
  `\90\39\5F\19\1A\B5\1B\4A\9E\96\86\C6\6C\B1\8D\11`
and
  `\x909_\x19\x1a\xb5\x1bJ\x9e\x96\x86\xc6l\xb1\x8d\x11`
are equivalent.  One is just a lot more intuitive for a human being to parse.

Your proposed change would provide a way to customize the textual representation of filters, though I'd suggest that the naming ("custom escape functionality") is perhaps a bit misleading.  "Custom LDAP filter textual representation" or "custom LDAP filter human-readable representation" both seem more accurate to me, though they are very wordy.

I wonder if it would be possible to push on the default  case a bit.  Do you think it might be possible to extend your idea so that the human-readable representation would automatically detect binary data in filters automatically represent *all* the octets with escape sequences?  Maybe if say half of the characters in the filter assertion value were non-printable?

I'll have access to an AD instance tomorrow, so I should be able to test to make sure that filtering on GUIDs already works-- it is just the textual representations can be queer-looking for binary data.

The SIS approach seems like it needs only one extra piece of information: a "new-master" key for the cluster repo.  The way it would work is:

1. manual client writes the "new-master" key.

2. the current master, at the beginning of its governor loop, checks for a new-master key.  If the new-master key isn't itself, it shuts down the postgreSQL server and releases the master lock key.

3. at the beginning of each replica's loop is a check for a new-master key.  If present, this key preempts all other failover logic.  If the key is present, that named server starts trying to acquire the master lock and become the master.

4. Once the new replica has become the master, it removes the new-master key.

Issues/additions:

a. the old master needs to check the new-master's metadata in SIS to make sure that it's able to fail over before shutting down.

b. we need some way to indicate to the user why manual failover did not occur if it fails.

c. if the new-master is unable to promote after the old master is shut down, what should the system do?  And how?

I have tested this steps. It works inside the virtualenv but then it is not fixed outside.

The bug is related to the detection of the `libs` in the [main script](https://github.com/SCons/scons/blob/master/src/script/scons.py). This `libs` are added to `sys.path` in order to `import SCons.Script`.

Since the virtualenv has only one version of Python, scons can detect and load the correct paths. Otherwise, if you have different versions of Python installed in your system, it can not detect the correct version for Python 3.5.

A solution is to setup the `SCONS_LIB_DIR` (which it is added to the `libs`) with the environment path of the *current apio version*. This is also good because apio will execute always *the correct scons*, in case there are multiple scons installed in the system.

You are calling "s0.translate(m_ctx)" in the worker thread.
This means that you are referencing a solver object s0 from potentially different threads at the same time.
This is not thread safe. Make sure to only touch derivatives from a context in a thread-safe way. 
A derivative of a context is any object created from it, except from the translate objects.
For example, a Solver object is created with respect to a context, expressions are created with respect to a context. They share state with the context object and this state cannot be accessed from different threads.

If you need to get proxy settings from environment variables -- use explicit function:
```
def proxy_from_env():
    proxy, proxy_auth = _parse(os.environ)
    return {'proxy': proxy, 'proxy_auth': proxy_auth}

async def something():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as sess:
        async with sess.get("https://httpbin.org/ip", **proxy_from_env()) as r:
            return (await r.json())
```

Let's discuss issues one by one.

1. Log names was modeled after Apache log format: http://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_log_config.html

2. Access Logger was not intended for inheritance, partially for historical reasons. That's why we don't use `self` in init but use class-level caching. The proper solution is moving a logger instantiation from `web.RequestHandler` to `web.Server` -- logger format parsing should be done once on server startup, not on every connection establishment.
We can even rewrite the `AccessLogger` completely from scratch keeping `AbstractAccessLogger` API. It may speed up log generation as well (the best solution is replacing logger with f-strings as shown in https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/logging.html#format-specification).

In my opinion, the `wait()`(maybe is not a good name) is a graceful `close()`, it should be same with `close()` for some behavior:
1. if `close()` can be allowed to call only once, `wait()`should be called only once
2. No jobs remain after `close()`, so `wait()` should wait all jobs, and it is what I need
3. All jobs include pending jobs, `wait()` should wait those jobs too

When ending a request context, one of the following can happen:
1) the connection can be immediately released to the connection pool if the body was read (no wait)
2) the connection has to be closed and a new connection created (body not read of client closed connection).  In this scenario ideally aiohttp would wait for the connection to close (it currently does not and thus can cause the number of open connections to keep growing..and yes I hit this before via SSL sockets).

Currently aiohttp does not wait but ideally in the future it will to keep the # of pool connections below the limit.

@pganssle pd.Period has a lot of functionality, most of which I agree is best left in pandas.  I'm suggesting parsing not-quite-dates into something other than a datetime.datetime object.  If you're dead-set against it, I can respect an aversion to feature creep.  But I'm still going to make a pitch for the idea.

> Assuming we say that the parser just returns the beginning of the period as a datetime

Under the status-quo, the default is to impute the current day:

`>>> dateutil.parser.parse('February 2009')`
`datetime.datetime(2009, 2, 15, 0, 0)`


This connotes more specificity than it should.  `pd.Period('February 2009')` represents this as `Period('2009-02', 'M')`.  Here it has inferred a frequency attribute 'M' which is not part of what I'm suggesting.

> How widely used are these formats? Do I need to include lookup maps for when the quarters are?

Most of the data from the Census, BLS, etc is either monthly or quarterly (i.e (Jan, Feb, Mar), (Apr, May, Jun), (Jul, Aug, Sep), (Oct, Nov, Dec)).  A Fiscal Calendar probably uses different quarters, but I'm happy to ignore that.

I had a go and implementing a formatter here: https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/8930/files (scroll down to the big block of green). Feel free to use any of the code there. It wasn't based off any standards, but was based off a StackOverflow answer I found somewhere.

The general idea behind it was to convert the `timedelta` to total seconds and calculate values in order of 'longest' units first, and use the remainder to calculate the next 'shortest' unit. eg. `timedelta(hours=1, minutes=0, seconds=3, microseconds=1e5)` converts to 3603.1s, and with a format of `'{H:02}:{M:02}:{S:02}'` the calculation is
```
hours, rem = divmod(3603.1, 3600)
# rem = 3.1
minutes, rem = divmod(rem, 60)
# rem = 3.1
seconds, rem = divmod(rem, 1)

seconds += rem
# hours = 1, minutes = 0, seconds = 3.1

returns '01:00:03.1'
```


In this implementation every unit apart from the smallest one is an integer.

For learning blocking, our objective is have blocks that lead to comparisons of "all true duplicates while minimizing **total** comparisons". This is a slightly different objective than "all true duplicates while minimizing comparisons of **distinct** records. In practice, they will be very similar because the vast bulk of possible comparisons will be of distinct records.

Because we are trying to minimize total comparisons, we can get an pretty accurate estimate of how many comparisons a block predicate will use by taking a random sample of all records and then blocking those. So, that's why we don't use distinct pairs.

Ok thanks, I just confirmed it. Note that it works if you use `--to dmrs-penman`. I believe the problem is that PyDelphin's implementation of EDS does not yet have a feature called ["predicate modification"](http://moin.delph-in.net/EdsTop#Graph_Connectivity) that connects "almost" and "all". DMRS retains scope information so it does not become disconnected, but EDS must resort to other means. The EDS you get from the SDP data was created with a different codebase that *does* perform predicate modification (note the ARG1 on the `_almost_x_deg`), so it's possible that you can convert to PENMAN using PyDelphin if you load the EDS representation and convert it directly to PENMAN, but currently `delphin.sh convert --from eds --to eds-penman` does not work because it requires round-tripping through a different representation. This is a deficiency in PyDelphin.

When I get a chance I'll see if I can resolve the EDS issue somehow. In the meantime you can try using DMRS instead of EDS, as PyDelphin has much better support for that representation.

I cannot think of any global data at this point. Can you think of anything?

Chain specific:
- block/state (one for lightchain and one for full chain) 
- private keys for accounts
- P2P node's private key (used to generate the node's unique ID and for authentication)
- peer statistics

Since all of that is actually _data_ (and not user-maintained _config_) generated/updated by py-evm, I believe it should go under _$XDG_DATA_HOME_ (which defaults to _$HOME/.local/share_).

As for the directory structure, I think the following would be a good start:

```
├── py-evm
│   └── mainnet
│       ├── chain         # LevelDB
│       ├── keys          # KeyStore, whatever format we chose
│       ├── lightchain    # LevelDB
│       ├── nodekey       # a text file containing the node's P2P private key
│       └── peers         # LevelDB
```

That also leaves room for any global data to be stored directly under _$XDG_DATA_HOME/py-evm_

Hi Randy,

There're at least two ways to approach this: theoretical (or rather rhetorical) and practical. From the theoretical point of view HMMs are parameteric models, in a sense that they make very specific assumptions about the way data is distributed:

* the distribution of the observations at time `t` is completely determined by the (unobserved) hidden state at time `t`,
* the sequence of hidden states forms a first-order Markov chain. 

The former also implies that you have to fix the family of distributions before doing inference. For instance, if you have reasons to believe that the continuous variables are normally distributed you need a `GaussianHMM`. Any other family (e.g. Gamma or Beta distributions) would give rise to a different HMM. Of course none of these assumptions hold perfectly for real-world data, so if you aren't sure, just go with Gaussians. 

This brings us to the practical approach, which is simply to go ahead and try the available models on your data. In general I wouldn't expect to much from HMMs, but they're still worth a try.

Another thing you'll probably need to decide on is the "meaning" of hidden states. I think for your type of problem you could go with two states: "good" and "poor" performance. Alternatively, you can introduce a number of intermediate states to differentiation between the varieties of the two. 

Hope this helps.

Right. If you customise the template locally, you wouldn't need to use `attrs`. When rendering, you can simply pass your attributes directly. Note that this example assume you are using Django 1.11. Say this is your custom template: This would be saved under `templates/markdownx/widget.html ``` html
{{ markdownx_editor }}
``` Then where you are intending to use it, say in your `index.html`, you would do this in your `my_app/views.py`: ``` python from my_app.models import MyModel class TestCreateView(CreateView): template_name = "index.html" model = MyModel success_url = '/' fields = [ 'markdownx_field1', 'markdownx_field2', 'textfield1', 'textfield2' ] def render_to_response(self, context, **response_kwargs): results, template, contenxt, using, kwargs = super(TestCreateView, self).render_to_response() context.update({ 'outer_editor_attrs': [ {'special_attr': 'value1'}, {'another_attr': 'value2'} ], 'outer_preview_attrs': [ {'prev_special_attr': 'val1'}, {'prev_another_attr': 'val2'} ] }) return results, template, context, using, kwargs ``` This will ensure that these attributes are include the way you want them to. If you want each editor/preview set in your page to have different special attributes, then you're gonna have to inherit the widget and override the attributes manually. Make sure you don't include another `class` attribute. If you want to do that, you need to do so by passing them to the widgets or there will be 2 `class` attributes in your template and browsers don't like that (not to mention that XHTML wouldn't let it pass anyway). I am not quite sure how useful this is to other people since you are the first person asking for it (and specially because when I wanted to do something like this, I resorted to using JavaScript myself - see the image). How about three progress bars in sequence, with these titles:

* Step 1/3: count local files
* Step 2/3: compare file lists
* Step 3/3: transfer data

Then it will know the number of file names that need to be compared in step 2, which can have a real progress bar.  Step 2 will take a little longer because it has to fetch file lists from B2 and get the sizes of local files. That syntax isn't bad for invariants. To summarize, this invariant would be treated as an *additional* constraint to the solver? E.g. not ignoring a current issue, but hinting to the solver that some false positive condition is actually not possible due to the constraint. So to comment here on some high level things without actually reviewing code:

* The redirect you’re seeing is coming from your browser, not our server. We do allow ingestion for this endpoint alone over both http and https. The protocol needs to match to avoid cross-origin requests, which strip headers that we need.
* Regardless of this change, raven-js takes it upon itself to strip the protocol anyways, no matter what we specify here for the above issue. So in theory, no change here will actually have an impact.
* Users run Sentry themselves in an environment that might not even have https, so we can enforce this on them.
There is no alternative that is secure. You just need to expose the secret and be willing to revoke it upon abuse.
The sentry client installs a hook by default via [sys.excepthook](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.excepthook)

Yes, that is expected, since AFAIK Django only allows one custom template to be declared per `ModelAdmin`. What I suggest you to do is merge both [django-advanced-filter's custom `change_list` template][1] and [django-import-export's custom `change_list` template][2] into [your own custom template][3] and declare it in your `ModelAdmin` class; or, if you intend to do this in multiple classes, a mixin like this can work:

```python
class CommonAdminMixin(ImportExportMixin, AdminAdvancedFiltersMixin):
    """
    Set a custom ``change_list_template`  to enable both `django-import-export`_ and
    `django-advanced-filters`_ functionality in the ``change_list`` admin view.
    """
    #: template for change_list view
    change_list_template = 'admin/change_list_common.html'
```

Unfortunately I don't know of any clean way to merge templates, you probably can just copy paste the content of import-export into advanced-filters and save in your template folder.

[1]: https://github.com/modlinltd/django-advanced-filters/blob/develop/advanced_filters/templates/admin/advanced_filters.html
[2]: https://github.com/django-import-export/django-import-export/blob/master/import_export/templates/admin/import_export/change_list_import.html
[3]: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/contrib/admin/#custom-template-options

J'ai essayé d'utiliser `cProfile` et `line_profiler` pour trouver des hotspots, sans succès.

## 1e essai : profiler tout

Copier le `run_test.py` de core puis lancer :

    python -m cProfile -o tests.cprof ./openfisca_france/scripts/test_runner.py --country-package openfisca_france tests
    gprof2dot -z simulations:132:calculate -n 25.0 -e 0 -f pstats tests.cprof | dot -Tpng -o cprof.png

![cprof](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/329236/41641421-85a1cda6-7465-11e8-9131-13d7967a7862.png)

Là on voit des choses gourmandes comme `activite:196:formula`.

## 2e essai :  _drill down_

Pour essayer d'en avoir une idée plus clare, nous ajoutons `with profile:` autour du code de la function, et lançons :

    kernprof -v -b -o tests.bprof ./openfisca_france/scripts/test_runner.py --country-package openfisca_france tests
    gprof2dot -n 25.0 -e 0 -f pstats tests.bprof | dot -Tpng -o bprof.png

![bprof](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/329236/41640936-dddb4224-7463-11e8-9306-88923d8ce69a.png)

Ce qui nous donne trois pistes possibles :

- le `base` des prélèvements obligatoires ;
- `entities` ou `simulations` sur core ; ou bien
- `simulations`, qui semble être un générateur (ce qui est cool normalement mais pas pour nos tests !)

### Générateur simulations

Finalement, le générateur a attiré mon attention, et je me suis posé la question de comment sont chargés et lus les fichiers `yaml` lors de la préparation de la suite de tests.

Ce que j'ai trouvé sur [openfisca-core/test_runner.py](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/079d83a7bf2be12223f9cda1ef76dacfecf71c71/openfisca_core/tools/test_runner.py) :

```python
def run_tests(tax_benefit_system, paths, options = {}):
    # ...
    return nose.run(
        # The suite argument must be a lambda for nose to run the tests lazily
        suite = lambda: generate_tests(tax_benefit_system, paths, options),
        argv = argv,
        )

# ...

def generate_tests(tax_benefit_system, paths, options = {}):
    # ...
    for path in paths:
        if os.path.isdir(path):
            for test in _generate_tests_from_directory(tax_benefit_system, path, options):
                yield test
        else:
            for test in _generate_tests_from_file(tax_benefit_system, path, options):
                yield test
```

Etc... donc on voit bien que les `yaml` sont chargés de façon `lazy`, et donc les `scenarios` sont crées au fil de l'eau.

To answer your question:

1. I think the current codebase does not support this feature. To disable rollback, we must specify `OnFailure='DO_NOTHING'` during stack creation. When we build the arguments for Cloudformation in `generate_cloudformation_args` in `~/providers/aws/default.py` file, we have not included this flag at all. Correct me if I'm wrong, but I think you cannot disable rollback for an existing stack.

I think it is possible to add support for this feature, reading environment variables would be a quick fix, but I don't know whether that's a good idea. I think it's more about design than the implementation.

2. I think the path is hardcoded in `stack_template_key_name` in `actions/base.py` file. If you are familiar with Python, I guess you could somehow override this function with a wrapper around stacker command. Again, this could be customisable, but I don't think it's a good idea. Mixing other things with cloudformation templates in a single bucket does not make sense to me, but that's just my personal opinion.

@librasungirl The Service Alocs handling & allocation will be much more convoluted after the changes you propose. But, yes, resource constraints might justify it. Namely, lets suppose such situation:

- `#define MAX_SERVICES_SUPPORTED 3`, it is equivalent to `mServiceAlocs[ 3 ]`
- node has service `5` and `6`. `5` sits in `mServiceAlocs[ 0 ]`, `6` is in `mServiceAlocs[ 1 ]`, `mServiceAlocs[ 2 ]` is unused (so some invalid address is there.
- new network data come in and leader for some reason decided to reassign both IDs, so our node's services are having IDs `3` and `4` now.
- I guess that the proper loop would be to a) remove unused services, b) add missing services, if possible
- so `for each srv aloc in mServiceAlocs` would realize that `mServiceAlocs[ 0 ]` is not necessary, remove it and filled with empty address, same for `mServiceAlocs[ 1 ]`. `mServiceAlocs[ 2 ]` would be skipped as containing bad address already
- then `for each service defined for this node` loop would need to allocate first empty `mServiceAlocs` entry for service `3`, then for `4`.

That can work, I think. It will iterate over service list more times, but it will spare on memory usage. I'll try to re-implement it that way.

I've tried to figure out how to fix this but it seems there's no way to handle this without breaking the xdist stuff.

There are two issues this would solve regarding paths like `.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/foobar`:
* Verbose report from pytest-cov. This is a minor esthetic issue - we can live with this.
* Incorrect paths in datafiles. This is a problem for publishing directly to codecov/coveralls. 

  *However*, this can easily be fixed with post-processing steps like `coverage combine --append`. 

I'm inclined to just give up on this and let users fix it up before publishing to coveralls/codecov (by running `coverage combine --append` or whatever). For reference, it seems to work, see [build](https://travis-ci.org/ionelmc/python-nameless) and [codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/ionelmc/python-nameless/commit/caf37a93d693b918e7c7db98aed7d1aeab006d29/builds) (note paths are alright in files tab) or [coveralls](https://coveralls.io/builds/9535515).

> Well thought @CAM-Gerlach! We should unify EOLs in new repos.

@ccordoba12 Excellent; really glad we're of the same mind.

> please create a file called .gitattributes

This is actually just what I did in the ``spyder-docs`` repo when I first created it: harmonized everything on LF and set up ``,gitattributes`` as you described, so I pulled over that ``.gitattributes`` as a starting point. I then probably went a *little* overboard explictly configuring it for every current or likely file type, to avoid any possibility of file corruption or missed inconsistent line endings due to git guessing wrong. However, the advantage is that we can use this same ``.gitattributes`` across all our repos with ease, since it should include every file type we are likely to encounter across any Spyder-related project, and thus we can port it over to ``website-spyder``, ``QtPy``, ``spyder-docs``, our plugins, etc. (and eventually the main repo itself, once we eventually break free of the CRLF curse there).

The one thing change from the above suggestion, as implemented in both the current ``.gitattributes`` on our ``spyder-docs`` repo and the one pushed here, is using ``* text=lf`` (with exceptions for Windows-specific files like ``.bat``), not ``* text=auto``, since the former will "maintain the same EOLs from now on" on all platforms and ensure the consistency and benefits mentioned above are present for all users, while the latter will actually make things worse for Windows users by trying to involuntarily convert *everything* (including files that should always stay LF) back to CRLF in their working tree, which furthermore for current users requires either a complicated series of steps or deleting their entire repository and starting fresh to prevent problems as the files are currently in their native line endings, i.e. LF except for those explicitly changed by this PR

Furthermore, this ensures consistent line endings across all platforms for all files, brings all the above-mentioned benefits of all-LF line endings to Windows users like myself, follows workflow best practices harmonizing on one line ending everywhere, avoids the overhead and complexity of constant conversion, makes the ``git diff`` output much cleaner (as it will constantly display spurious <kbd>^M</kbd> symbols on any changed lines), ensures the working tree is always consistent with the repo itself.

The other error above is from installation of `cryptography`, a dependency of `paramiko`, which requires openssl development headers package to build its native extensions (``openssl/opensslv.h: No such file or directory``).  These are in `libssl-dev` package on debian derived systems. Error is not related to `parallel-ssh`.

The `pyopenssl` package otoh provides bindings to the openssl libraries as pure python code and is a (slower) alternative to having to install openssl development headers.

In short, either install `libssl-dev` or equivalent package so that cryptography can build its native extensions or use `pyopenssl`. Cryptography by default will try to build native extensions against openssl development headers.

Note that cryptography with native extensions without pyopenssl is likely to be faster and with fewer python package dependencies.

From my system with `libssl-dev` installed installing cryptography pulls in:

```
Successfully installed cffi-1.9.1 cryptography-1.7.1 enum34-1.1.6 idna-2.2 ipaddress-1.0.17 pyasn1-0.1.9 pycparser-2.17 six-1.10.0
```

For some directions down in that hole, have a look at the [pynput implementation of key resolving](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/keyboard/_xorg.py#L373) (using [this](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/xorg.py#L319) rather simple function).

@J0WI Sorry, I missed your last comments. Pinning the root cert is not useless, it prevents any other (compromised) CAs issue a cert for your site.
If you have pinned the root cert, and a hacker want to MITM attack your website without being noticed by normal users, the hacker has to compromise the CA that issued your cert, instead of any CAs in user's OS/browser's trust list (which could be hundreds CAs) , this make the hacker's life much harder.

lighttpd handles vhost almost like apache, on ubuntu it has the same setup like
`/etc/lighttpd/site-enabled/domain.com (ln link)`
`/etc/lighttpd/site-available/domain.com`
`/etc/lighttpd/lighttpd.conf (global settings)`

so you can generate a domain.com-ssl.conf in `/etc/lighttpd/site-available/` and link it in `/etc/lighttpd/site-enabled/`

there is one important thing
the input pem has to be a combined privkey+cert.pem

its simple to make, and put in a program so that it outputs a lighttpd compatible file out of the box
`sudo su (login as root)`
`cd /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com`
`cat privkey.pem cert.pem > ssl.pem`

```
 $SERVER["socket"] == ":443" {

     ssl.engine = "enable"
     ssl.pemfile                 = "/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/ssl.pem"
     ssl.ca-file                 =  "/etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/fullchain.pem"
     server.document-root = "/home/web-secure/public_html"
 }
```

I thought of another complication: there's some subtle interaction between `MultiError` and this feature. Specifically, there are two fairly common cases to think about:

* The cancel scope gets a `MultiError` containing multiple `Cancelled` exceptions, and wants to catch them all. (This is the usual thing that happens if you cancel a block that contains a nursery with multiple running tasks in it.)

* The cancel scope gets a `MultiError` containing a mix of `Cancelled` exceptions and other things, and wants to catch (some of) the `Cancelled` exceptions and let the rest propagate.

Right now, `MultiError.catch` maps a handler over all the exceptions inside a `MultiError`, and lets you examine each one before deciding whether to catch it or not. Then it discards all the ones that were caught, and if any are left it re-raises a new exception built out of them. (With some cleverness to keep tracebacks correct etc.)

So the simple way to do this would be to have our handler check the flag, and if it's set, then for each primitive `Cancelled` exception it catches, it stashes it in some set or something. But the problem with this is that you lose the tree structure and traceback that the original `MultiError` had -- you just get a flat list of exceptions with part of their tracebacks missing. Not really something that you want to attach as a `__context__`. In the second case above, this is perhaps the best you can manage... I guess we could have some mechanism for `MultiError.catch` to also extract the caught exceptions and reconstruct them into a proper `MultiError`?

Another option, a bit hackier but maybe simpler if it works, would be to say oh well, we actually only care about capturing these exceptions when we're in the first case above. So what we could do is have an explicit check where we save the original `MultiError`, run the `MultiError.catch` as usual, and then if it swallows everything we know that the original `MultiError` consisted solely of our `Cancelled` exceptions and we just save that instead. But this does lead to some weird inconsistencies – we set the "did we catch an exception?" flag based on whether we caught at least one `Cancelled` (i.e., it's true in both of the cases above), but if we did things this way then we'd only actually have an exception to point to as being the one we caught in the first case, not the second.

Can you try `ray.register_custom_serializer`? The following works for me.

```python
import ray
ray.init()

class Graph:
    def __init__(self):
        self.g = self

ray.register_custom_serializer(Graph, use_pickle=True)

G = Graph()
ray.put(G)
```

This is closely related to #319 and https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/ARROW-1382.

A side comment. The original code worked for me in Python 2 because in Python 2 `Graph` is an old-style class and so we automatically fall back to Pickle anyway I think.

Trying to formalize the autoscaling algo here. @almussel, this is modified from what we agreed to yesterday.

The central data structure is a mapping that maps a sample ID to the number of nodes currently needed for that sample. We assume that the data structure is persistent, can be updated atomically, and with strong consistency and is shared among all Toil workers as well as the head node that's running the überscript. To synchronize updates to that data structure, optimistic locking could be used (aka conditional updates). A single row in a SimpleDB domain fits those requirements.

A sample-specific job that adds one or more children occupying entire nodes modifies the mapping by atomically adding the required number of nodes in the entry for the current sample. The invariant is that the sum of all values in the mapping is the desired overall cluster size.

The überscript monitors the mapping and periodically adds instances if the desired cluster size is less than the current cluster size. Nodes that are detected as idle will be removed only if the current cluster size is greater than the desired cluster size.

Option: There is the possibility of some lower than required number of nodes being added and those nodes being divided among multiple pending, per-sample Spark sub-clusters. Neither of these sub-clusters would be complete and the new nodes would sit idle. To fix this, each Toil job that allocates additional nodes in the above manner should block until its nodes become available. To do so, it loops over the entries in the mapping in the lexicographical order of the keys, summing the values until it hits its own entry in the mapping. If, at that time, it detects that the running sum is less than the current cluster size, it schedules its children. Otherwise it sleeps and retries. The current cluster size is a globally visible value maintained by the überscript, probably another row in the same SimpleDB domain that contains the mapping. This option is the only reason that we need a complex mapping and not just a scalar representing the desired cluster size. The deterministic ordering of the iteration avoids having to track a mapping between individual nodes and samples while still being able to arbitrate between multiple blocked jobs.

Problems: Doesn't model multiple subclusters per sample but maybe we don't need that.

@glyph wrote: 
>  If .path is "the segments as they appear between slashes" then it's easy to establish the invariant that the strings in it can't contain slashes.

OK, with this approach I cannot add a sub-path to the segment as `some/path/to` in one step, I have to add multiple path segments in turn. Let's trace through the impacts of allowing adding such sub-paths or not allowing it:

If you allow adding a sub-path then you need to accept that sometimes it will have slashes in and sometimes it might have %-encoded slashes that are intended to be retained as such. In which case you really have to trust that the providing code was written by someone who knew what they were doing. And then when you pass that string on somewhere and it is decoded then any sub-paths with slashes in will be itemised separately.

Alternatively if you do require each segment to be appended separately then the API needs to be  clear about what it is going to do if there is a reserved character in it - will it _always_ percent encode the provided string, or will it _never_ percent encode it but _always_ reject an incorrectly encoded string?

accessed such as "coords" which is an array of all the pixel coordinates 
within a labeled region, and "perimeter" which is the computed length of 
the region perimeter.  However there is no property for the perimeter 
pixel coordinates.

would be needed internally in skimage.measure.regionprops to provide 
some other properties such as those listed above.  Since the data is 
already there internally it would be useful if it were made available as 
a property.

Something like:

perimeter_coords: (N,2) ndarray

Ordered array of the pixel coordinates (x, y) along the perimeter of the 
region.

I am only learning python and am not familiar with the internals of 
skimage.  If you do come up with an implementation of exposing 
"perimeter_coords" I would be very interested in seeing your code. It 
would help me learn so that maybe someday I could also contribute.

scikits-image.

You can have multiple schema - one per app - if you define each app `Query` as an `AbstractType`, and then in your main project schema you can import them and include each one in the 'master' `Query` class through multiple inheritance of all the app `Query` classes.

My vote goes to **deprecation**. The main use-case for `only-once` is a handler that isn't idempotent, such as formatting HDFS.

- Non-idempotent handlers are already an anti-pattern since a handler might run multiple times during debugging.
- The `only_once` functionality can be simulated by using states if you really need it.

Okay, so it looks like you more or less have something that would work. Here's a few things about xmlchemy/oxml.

So the CT_FooBar classes are lxml _custom element classes_. That means they're registered (in oxml.__init__.py as you've done) and the lxml parser uses those classes to construct the matching elements.

What's not so evident until you dig is that the base class for those, `BaseOxmlElement` or something, is a _metaclass_ (or derives from one). This metaclass allows you to give objects of a custom element class methods, just by specifying something like `x = OneOrMore('w:x', ...)` or `y = RequiredAttribute('w:y', ...)`. Basically it makes definition of standard methods _declarative_ and generally saves a lot of typing and testing.

Each one of those `OneOrMore()` assignments (and its brothers ZeroOrMore, OneAndOnlyOne(), etc.) cause the class to have a certain standard set of methods. In the case of `x = ZeroOrOne('w:x', ...)`, objects of the class would have `.x`, a property that returns that element (or None if not present), `.get_or_add_x()` which adds an x first if it's not there, `_remove_x()` which gets rid of it, and also a couple more internal methods like `._new_x()` and `._insert_x()` which usually aren't used unless you're getting fancy. You can do a `dir(...)` on one of those element objects to see what methods and properties it gets.

Now the limitation with those automatic methods is that they can take no parameters, at least none specific to the structure of the element. So if you wanted `CT_P.add_bookmark_start(name)`, you have to write it yourself, using the elementary building blocks provided by xmlchemy, perhaps something like this:

```python
class CT_P(...):
    ...
    def add_bookmark_start(self, name):
        bookmarkStart = self.add_bookmarkStart()
        bookmarkStart.name = name
        bookmarkStart.id = self._next_id  # ---or its equivalent, not sure yet
```

So the short version is, if you need something simple, you probably get it for free with your declaration (e.g. `x = OneOrMore('w:x', ...)`) and if you need something fancier, you build it up from what you get for free.

Let me know if that helps and where in particular you're stuck on a next step (as briefly as possible :) and we'll go from there.

Btw, the XML Schema Excerpts section in the doc needs some refining and simplification; I can't make out what attributes they need and what are required. Unnecessary levels should be condensed. The point with the excerpts is not to be complete or exactly the same as the actual schema, but rather to communicate clearly what the elements you need look like, what children they can have and what attributes they have and the values they can take.

You can see sometimes in other analysis docs (maybe more often in python-pptx) where the term `<!-- denormalized -->` appears where a level of indirection has been removed. The MS schema is highly abstracted to allow reuse of certain objects, but that can make it hard to follow in a particular instance when understanding is your aim rather than technical generality. So I'll "expand" references in-line so to speak so you can get a better feel when reading the excerpt. In particular, I don't see where the `w:id` attribute is defined and don't get where the colFirst and colLast fit in. No, PySCIPOpt itself does not implement multi-threading. Parallel processing within SCIP is still experimental (you might want to check out https://ug.zib.de for some massive parallelization using SCIP).

PySCIPOpt is also completely unaware of the used LP solver within SCIP. This solely depends on how you built the SCIP library that PySCIPOpt is linked against. So, yes, it's completely fine to use SCIP/Gurobi with PySCIPOpt. Information on how to build the library with a different LP solver than SoPlex is provided in the SCIP documentation (set the `LPS` parameter to e.g. `grb`).

First of all, long-term I'm against using namespace packages. Short-term, I see both pros and cons of temporarily introducing them. 

Technically I don't think anything that prevents us from making ``invenio.modules`` into a namespace package. Only ``invenio.modules.__init__`` needs to be empty (i.e with the namespace package declaration), which it currently is.

Cons:
- Namespace packages have lots of installation pitfalls (speaking by experience having managed a large application using namespace packages). Our installation procedure will likely guard against this, but if a user installs it any other way you risk ending up with a pretty messed up ``site-packages``.
- We're just postponing the pain - the work of fixing imports has to be done at some point.
- Risk of staying with namespace packages.

Pros:
- Faster migrationer due to less up-front work.
- Possible to perhaps externalise a module, and let it die (i.e. not having to do the migration).

> invenio/__init__.py: not loaded anymore (so no invenio.__version__, use invenio.version.__version__ instead

Just a side comment, that I don't agree with this. Current practice is that ``invenio.__init__`` contains a line ``from .version import __version__`` so that version can be accessed by ``from invenio import __version__``. The version is in a separate file, so that it can be easily read and parsed without loading the entire project by e.g. ``setup.py``.

Hi @mraduldubey and @hyonschu,

The command line option for the `quantize` mode are a bit confusing:
 - `-input` is used to specify the data that will be used for fine-tuning the quantize model. This file is only used if the `-retrain` option is set, and should be the same as the original training data. Without fine-tuning, this path is not used.
 - `-output` is used to specify the path to the model **without** the `.bin` extension.
Here is an example of command lines to train and then quantize a model:
```
> ./fasttext supervised -input DATA.TXT -output MODEL -wordNgrams 2 -dim 50
> ./fasttext quantize -input DATA.TXT -output MODEL -dsub 2
```
Please note that only supervised models can be quantized for now.

Best,
Edouard.

I like this version without `fit` and `feature_labels` turned off the best, and am fine with merging it after addressing:

- Should we change the docstring of `featurize_dataframe` to describe what the operation does rather than how it was implemented (i.e., just copy the one from BaseFeaturizer)?
- I am concerned about the compatibility of "`new_df.columns = df.columns.tolist() + new_col_names`" with Python 2.7 (and possibly before 3.6, which introduced using insertion-ordered dicts by default). My worry is that the new columns will always be the last ones in the dataframe after running `featurize_dataframe`. If this is indeed a problem [I'll do some tests tomorrow, don't have time now], I have a few ideas for addressing it.

To make sure we're in consensus, my reasoning for why I like it is below.

Thanks for putting this together. I really like how flexible FunctionFeaturizer is, and I look forward to trying it out soon!

---------

The main advantage I could think of for storing the feature names/number of input features with `fit` is providing warnings if the number of features changes, which does not seem very useful. I agree, it is much nicer to be able to avoid using `fit` altogether. 

The `featurize_dataframe` still works in the same way as in the other featurizers: what features and how they are computed will not change after subsequent evaluations (unless the input features are changed, which should certainly change the output). So, I'm on board with overriding it.

This implementation will also work nicely in Pipeline objects. The way I plan to use this to put it in a pipeline after other featurizers. Something like:

```python
p = Pipeline([
    ('featurizers', ElementProperty().from_preset('magpie')),
    ('expander', FunctionFeaturizer())
])
```

In short, I could still call `p.fit_transform` and expect it to return features, and `transform` and expect none of the configurations of the featurizers to change.

There is no way to document a dynamic default: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/554.

What we should do is allow the user to opt-out of default documenting. Then he can either specify the default behaviour in the description or add a `x-something` attribute.

So we need an attribute meaning "don't document default". `default_doc` is not really a good name for that.

From the discussion in https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/554, datetime is the only common use case. Not documenting callable defaults is not really an option, as we'll miss `dict`, `list` and such.

When I have run into a problem like this in the past it is because I was 
using a newer version of Django that created both a project level and 
application level directories.  The top level contained the manage.py 
file and the lower level contains the setup.py and is where I placed the 
models.py which I created using inspectdb (in this example the project 
and app are named "ds"):

>     $ ls -R
>     db.sqlite3    ds        ds2        manage.py
>
>     ./ds:
>     __init__.py    models.py    settings.py    urls.py wsgi.py

When I tried to filter on objects (Cases, Accounts, etc) I would get the 
error you noted.  I determined the problem was that I needed to include 
my app directory name in list for "INSTALLED_APPS" in settings.py.

We are seeing a non-trivial number of failures of `Connection.delete_bucket(force=True)`:
https://travis-ci.org/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/builds/46346592#L329
https://travis-ci.org/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/builds/42891293#L261

-----

This failure is because, `Connection.delete_bucket()`:

    
1.  [Loops through][1] a list of objects in the bucket.
1.  This creates a new [`_KeyIterator`][2] which gets a list of all the objects from the API (since [`__iter__`][4] does not bind anything to the `Bucket` object, a new iterator is returned every time 

    ```python
    for key in bucket:
        ...
    ```
    occurs, hence a fresh API request to list the objects)
1.  Tries to [delete][3] each retrieved key, some of which no longer exist. (hence the 404) This seems to indicate that the '/bucket-name/o/` request is returned stale data. (hence the desire to talk to eng.)

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/1bfa469f8a18c92f7b3e5a351eb955b48afdd1f1/gcloud/storage/connection.py#L478
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/1bfa469f8a18c92f7b3e5a351eb955b48afdd1f1/gcloud/storage/bucket.py#L29
[3]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/1bfa469f8a18c92f7b3e5a351eb955b48afdd1f1/gcloud/storage/connection.py#L479
[4]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/1bfa469f8a18c92f7b3e5a351eb955b48afdd1f1/gcloud/storage/bucket.py#L110

I can confirm it also holds for `pubsub`. I'm addressing for now by manually corralling all the child processes via [`psutil`][1]

```python
proc = psutil.Process(pid)
for child_proc in proc.children(recursive=True):
    try:
        child_proc.kill()
        child_proc.terminate()
    except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
        pass
proc.terminate()
proc.kill()
```

You're running the diff the wrong way around. GitPython follows the order of arguments to that command line git diff would also require: old -> new. Extends the existing functionality of staging "writable" files to the output directory.  If `inplaceUpdate` is true, files will link (via symlink or bind mount) to the real files and permit updating.

Enforces sequencing of updates by incrementing a "generation" number with each update (*).  Files can only be update by one workflow step at a time, and the updated file must be passed explicitly to downstream steps.  If two steps try to update a file of the same generation it will raise an error.

(*) In writing this, I realized that while write-write conflicts are detected, read-write conflicts are not.  So the lock management probably needs a bit more work.

Ah yes that changed slightly, because it is an extension it needs to be prefixed:

```
$namespaces:
  cwltool: http://commonwl.org/cwltool#
requirements:
  cwltool:InplaceUpdateRequirement:
    inplaceUpdate: true
```

Regarding caching, I remembered that it does record the file size and modified time of each input file as part of the cache (although it currently doesn't descend into directories, which is a bug).  So if a workflow step takes as input a file that was destructively modified later on (updating the mtime) then it won't reuse that step from cache because the input file is different.  On the other hand, if an upstream step produces an output file which is modified in place by a downstream step, that contaminates the output of the upstream step, so the upstream step probably shouldn't be reused either.

Maintaining correctness when applying destructive updates is hard.
